vi 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
PREFACE 
 
The Agricultural Science Ordinary Level Teaching Module has been developed to provide guidance to the 
teacher on how to deliver competence-based lessons which are age-appropriate for learners at Form 2. The 
teaching module is aimed at providing quality education that is aligned with the Competence-Based Curriculum 
and 21st Century Skills. It also aims to facilitate the building of knowledge, skills, values and positive attitudes that 
are aimed at enabling children to live and grow into productive and useful members of their communities and 
the Zambian society.  
 
This teaching module is interconnected to the Form one module. The guide provided in this teaching module is 
aimed at facilitating the acquisition of knowledge, competences and values to nurture a learner who is creative, 
critical and an analytical thinker. The suggested learning activities are designed to offer learners hands-on and 
minds-on experiences through manipulation of real objects, interaction with nature and learning through 
activities. Thus, the learner will develop knowledge, desirable lifelong skills, values and positive attitudes needed 
for their personal and national development.  
 
It is hoped that the guidance provided in this module will make learning of Agricultural Science at Ordinary level 
more meaningful and enjoyable as it is highly activity oriented and allows for a smooth transition into the 
Constructivist way of learning.  
 
 
 
 
 
Kelvin Mambwe (PhD.)  
Permanent Secretary- Educational Services  
MINISTRY OF EDUCATION

viii 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
INTRODUCTION 
 
The Agricultural Science Ordinary Level Teaching Module has been developed to guide teachers on how to 
facilitate competence-based lessons at this level. The module aims to provide quality education that is aligned 
with the competence-based curriculum and 21st Century Skills, that is developing knowledge, skills, values and 
positive attitudes that are aimed at enabling Learners to live and grow into productive and useful members of 
their communities and the Zambian society. The syllabus presents Agricultural Science as an applied science. 
Agricultural science will be taught using learner centered activities which will help leaners to acquire desirable 
lifelong skills, values and positive attitudes needed for their personal and national development.  
Agricultural science will be taught using learner centred activities, which will help learners to acquire desirable 
lifelong skills, values and positive attitudes needed for their personal and national development. Agricultural 
Science, as an applied science, will be taught in a practical manner, in which learners will be involved in carrying 
out agricultural activities, which will include growing crops, rearing poultry, keeping livestock, apiculture and 
aquaculture. Therefore, Agricultural Science activities will be done both in the laboratory where experiments will 
be conducted and, in the field, where field trials/observations will be done. The assessment in the teaching 
module is part of the school-based assessment. It will be emphasized by using various methods of testing 
according to sub-topics as part of the normal teaching and learning process and therefore, the assessment 
opportunities will also occur during this normal process.  
The assessment to measure each Specific Competence will occur in three forms:  
 Observation – watching learners working (good for assessing skills and values)  
 Conversation – asking questions and talking to learners (good for assessing knowledge and understanding 
 Product – appraising the learners’ work (writing reports, translation, calculations, presentations, maps, 
diagrams, models etc.) in this context the product is seen as something physical and permanent that the 
teacher can keep and look at.

ix 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
PURPOSE OF THE MODULE 
 
The purpose of the module is to illustrate teaching and learning activities that: 
i. are consistent with teaching and learning in a competence-based curriculum, 
ii. show processes that allow achievement of 21st Century Skills such as creativity, collaboration, 
communication and critical thinking, 
iii. illustrate what activity-based and learner-centred teaching and learning could look like.

x 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
HOW TO USE THE MODULE 
 
This module provides suggested learning activities and teaching strategies that enhance learners’ scientific 
competences. The suggested activities are designed to offer learners hands-on experiences through 
manipulation of real objects, interaction with nature and learning through practice, bearing in mind that the 
desired competences and 21st century skills will NOT be achieved by the topics ‘covered’ but how the teaching 
and learning activities are carried out.  
Activities in the Module are meant to be incorporated in the teacher’s lesson plan. Thus, the activities are NOT 
meant to replace the teacher’s lesson plan. 
The topics and activities in the Module are as specified in the 2024 Agricultural Science syllabus. There might be 
terms used in the Module that are not the same as those in the syllabus, but this is in the interest of using language 
that might be more appropriate for the learner. Teachers are encouraged to be mindful as well about what 
language is within the grasp of their learners and not use terminology simply because it appears in the syllabus. 
Teachers are encouraged to start topics with a hook that provokes their learners’ curiosity and motivation. A 
Hook is an opening statement at the start of the topic that seeks to stimulate learners’ curiosity and motivate 
them to learn.  A hook can be a provocative question, a scenario or something that puts learners into a thinking 
mood. Let the designed hook incline towards problem-solving scenarios.

TOPIC 2.1.0: AGRICULTURE IN ZAMBIA  
 
 Introduction  
This topic focuses on land tenure and land use. Learners will explore existing land tenure systems and analyze the effects of land tenure 
and human population growth on agricultural development. In addition, learners will investigate the factors that limit land use in different 
regions of Zambia and explore the various forms of land use practiced in the country. 
 
 
General Competences   
• Analytical thinking,   
• Communication,  
• Creativity and innovation,  
• Collaboration,   
 
• Critical thinking,  
• Environmental sustainability and  
• Problem solving. 
Hook: “Do you know who owns land in your community and do you know that you can own land for 99 years?

2 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
 
  
 
Customary land - 
land that is owned and managed according to traditional customs and practices of a local community rather 
than through formal government land titles or statutory law. 
Commercial farming - 
type of farming where crops and livestock are produced for sale in the market to make profit rather than 
for family consumption. 
Freehold -  
a form of land ownership where a person owns the land permanently and has full legal rights to use, sell, 
lease or transfer it to heirs. 
Land tenure -  
 
is the condition or period under which an occupier holds land. 
Land use -  
 
 
the management and modification of natural land resources for human activities such  
as farming, housing or industry. 
Lease hold- 
A form of property ownership where a person (tenant or leaseholder) has the right to use and occupy land 
or building for a fixed period. 
State land -   
 
Land owned and controlled by the state or government. 
Subsistence farming-  
Type of farming where crops and livestock are kept mainly for family consumption and not for sale. 
Traditional leaders-  
Individuals such as chiefs, headmen, village leaders who govern and guide communities according to 
customs, traditions and cultural norms. 
 
 
Key Concepts / Processes / Terms

3 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
Sub-Topic 2.1.1 Land Tenure and Land Use 
Introduction   
In this sub-topic, learners are going to investigate land tenure systems and land use in Zambia.  
Specific Competence   
Interpret the land tenure systems and land use in Zambia. 
Learning Activities   
The following are some of the activities you can use to teach the learners about the land tenure systems and land use in Zambia. 
1.1.1 Describing land tenure and land use 
Learning materials: Textbooks, Notebook, manilla paper, digital and print media. 
 
Activity 1: Describing land tenure and land use 
Procedure 
i. 
Divide learners into small groups of 3-4 
ii. 
Ask each group to discuss the terms; land tenure and land use. 
iii. 
Ask each group to brainstorm on who owns land in their community and how land is used in the school or homes. 
iv. 
Ask each group to take notes and write their findings on the manilla papers. 
v. 
Ask each group to present their findings. 
Supporting Activities 
i. 
Viewing video clips on land tenure and land use.

4 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
ii. 
Inviting officers from Ministry of Lands or traditional leaders. 
 
1.1.2 Exploring existing land tenure systems (Communal, customary, leasehold and state land …) 
Learning Materials: Maps of Zambia showing land use and land ownership, Charts on land tenure systems, Pictures of different land 
uses, atlases and textbooks.  
Activity 1: Exploring existing land tenure systems (Communal, customary, leasehold and state land …) 
Procedure: 
i. Divide learners into small groups of 3-4.   
ii. Ask learners to use maps, charts, pictures and textbooks related to land tenure and land use as provided by the teacher. Learners 
to identify and list the main land tenure systems in Zambia. 
iii. Ask learners to discuss how each land tenure system influences land use (e.g., grazing, farming, settlement). 
iv. Ask each group to prepare short description of their findings and make presentations. 
                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
1.1.2 Exploring existing land tenure systems (Communal, customary, leasehold and state land …) 
Learning Materials: Charts/Posters showing types of land tenure systems, Maps of Zambia indicating land ownership and use, Flash 
cards, Picture/photographs, Case study handouts, Chalkboard, Work cards/sheets for group work. 
Activity 2. Exploring existing land tenure systems (Communal, customary, leasehold and state land…)

5 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
Procedure:  
i. Divide learners into small groups of 3-4. 
ii. Assign each group with one land tenure system. 
iii. Ask learners to discuss the assigned land tenure system (focusing on meaning, ownership and uses). 
iv. Ask each group to prepare poster or written summary of land tenure assigned. 
v. Ask learners to present their findings. 
 
1.1.2 Exploring existing land tenure systems (Communal, customary, leasehold and state land …) 
Activity 3: Study tour 
Procedure: 
i. 
Divide learners into small groups of 3-4 pupils. 
ii. 
Ask learners to visit a nearby community to investigate the system of land tenure in the locality and how the system influences 
agricultural productivity. 
iii. 
Ask groups to present findings in class. 
Content Tip:  
 This procedure promotes collaboration, research skills, communication and understanding of land tenure systems and land uses. 
Supporting Activities 
i. 
Role play  
ii. 
Viewing video clips on land tenure systems

6 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
 
1.1.3 Analysing the effects of land tenure and human population on agriculture  
Learning Materials: Notebooks, questionnaires 
Activity 1. Analysing the effects of land tenure and human population on agriculture  
Procedure: 
i. 
Divide learners into small groups of 3-4 pupils. 
ii. 
Ask learners to visit local farmers in the nearby community. Learners should ask farmers whether they own the land they 
are using for production.  
iii. 
Ask learners to find out from farmers the challenges they face in relation to use of land with an increase in human 
population. 
iv. 
Ask groups to present findings in class. 
1.1.3 Analysing the effects of land tenure and human population on agriculture 
Activity 2: Scenario  
A village has experienced rapid population growth. Land that once had large farms is now divided into smaller ones. 
i. 
Put learners into small groups of 3-4 pupils. 
ii. 
Ask learners to discuss the following questions in relation to the scenario presented above; 
 What problems may arise for farmers due to rapid population growth? 
 How does this affect food production in the community? 
 Suggest solutions that could help mitigate these challenges? 
iii. 
Ask learners to present their findings.

1.1.4 Investigating factors that limit the use of land in different areas of Zambia  
Learning Materials: Notebooks, Map of Zambia 
Activity 1. Investigating factors that limit the use of land in different areas of Zambia 
Procedure 
i. Put learners into small groups of 3-4.  
ii. Ask learners to visit a nearby farming area or open land. 
iii. Ask learners to identify factors that limit the use of land in different areas visited. 
iv. Ask learners to report their findings. 
Content Tips 
 
Poor soils, steep slopes, floods, lack of water, drought, population, soil fertility, pests, diseases, land policies, wildlife, thick 
forest as factors that limit use of land.

 
Flood plains such as the Barotse Floodplain limit permanent farming because of seasonal floods. 
 
Conservation areas like South Luangwa National Park restrict agriculture to protect wildlife. 
Supporting material 
 Consulting print and digital resources

Supporting Activities 
i.

Skills Developed 
 Communicating – Learners sharing their findings with peers and sharing descriptions of land tenure and land use. 
 Collaborating – Learners working together in groups to explore existing land tenure systems in Zambia.  
 Analytically Thinking – Learners analysing the effects of land tenure and human population on agriculture. 
 Problem Solving – Understanding factors that limit the use of land in different areas in order to create possible solutions for

Assessment

12 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
The tasks in this section are just samples. A teacher is free to give own assessment activities. 
Task 1 
In Isoko Village, community members engage in various economic activities such as growing vegetables and field crops, rearing 
poultry, piggery, cattle, goats, and other livestock. Recently, the exploration and discovery of valuable mineral resources in the area 
has increased demand for land. As a result, there is growing competition for land among mining companies, farmers, government 
institutions, and potential investors planning development projects. Due to this situation, the local leadership has organized a 
community sensitization program to help residents understand how land should be owned, managed, and used responsibly. As a 
learner who has studied land management concepts, you have been invited to participate in educating the community. 
Prepare a short presentation for the community sensitization program addressing the following:  
i. Explain the meaning of term ‘land tenure’; 
ii. Describe the main types of land tenure systems practiced in Zambia; 
iii. Outline the key characteristics of each land tenure system, and  
iv. Discuss how proper understanding of land tenure and land use can help reduce conflicts and promote sustainable development 
in the community. 
Task 2 
The Ministry of Lands and Natural Resources has organized an inter-school paper presentation aimed at improving learners’ 
understanding of land management and ownership systems in Zambia. The event will bring together learners from different schools to 
present and discuss important issues related to land administration and sustainable land use. As a learner who has demonstrated strong 
knowledge in land tenure and land use, your school has selected you to represent it at this event. You are required to prepare a paper 
presentation that will help fellow learners understand how land is owned, accessed, and managed in Zambia. 
Prepare a well-structured presentation in which you: 
i. Explain the meaning of term ‘land use’; 
ii. Identify and describe the main types of land tenure systems practiced in Zambia;

13 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
iii. Discuss how human population growth influences agricultural land use, and 
iv. Discuss the advantages of each land tenure system in promoting land security and sustainable development. 
Task 3 
The District Agricultural Coordinating Officer has organized a District Conference on Land Use aimed at discussing challenges and 
opportunities related to land management and agricultural development in local communities. The conference will bring together 
farmers, agricultural officers, community leaders, and learners to share knowledge and propose solutions for improving land 
utilization. As a learner with wide knowledge of land tenure, the effects of human population on agriculture, factors that limit land use 
in Zambia, and different forms of land use based on agricultural systems and farming practices, you have been selected to prepare a 
report that will be presented during the conference. 
Prepare a well-organized report for presentation at the conference in which you: 
i. Identify and explain the major factors that limit land use in Zambia; 
ii. Describe the different forms of land use based on agricultural systems and farming practices; 
iii. Suggest possible measures that communities can adopt to promote sustainable and efficient use of land, and 
iv. Analyse different forms of land use based on agricultural systems and farming practices. 
Sub-Topic 2.1.1 Summary  
In Zambia, land tenure systems determine how land is owned and managed. The main types are customary, communal, state land, 
freehold and leasehold land ownership. Understanding land tenure and land use helps plan agriculture and manage resources 
effectively

14 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
TOPIC 2.2.0 SOIL SCIENCE 
Introduction 
This sub-topic covers soil fertility. Soil fertility is the ability of soil to provide essential nutrients to plants for healthy growth. Fertile 
soil contains the right balance of nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium and other minerals. Learners will engage in demonstrating 
understanding of the nutrient cycles through analysing and illustrating nitrogen and carbon cycles in relation to soil fertility. 
General Competencies 
 Analytical thinking  
 Communication  
 Creativity and innovation  
 Collaboration 
 Critical thinking 
 Problem solving 
 Environmental sustainability 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Hook: “Do you know that even if soil may look ordinary, it is hidden treasure full of nutrients, water and life. Today, 
we will uncover its secrets.”

15 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
 
 
Ammonification -  
is the process by which microorganisms (bacteria and fungi) break down dead plants, animals and waste materials’ 
convent organic nitrogen into ammonia (NH3) or ammonium (NH4+) in the soil. 
Denitrification -  
is the process in which bacteria convert nitrates (N03-) in the soil into nitrogen gas (N2), which is then released 
back into the atmosphere. 
Fossilization -  
is the process by which the remains or traces of plants and animals from the past are preserved in rocks, forming 
fossils over a long period of time. 
Organic matter -  
Decomposed plant and animal materials in the soil that improves fertility. 
Nitrogen fixation -  the process by which nitrogen from the air is converted into forms usable by plants often by bacteria. 
Nitrification -  
is the conversion of ammonia or ammonium into nitrites (NO2-) and then into nitrates (N03-) by nitrifying bacteria 
in the soil. 
Compost manure -  is organic fertiliser made from decomposed plant materials, food waste and animal waste which is added to soil 
to improve its fertility and structure. 
Soil fertility -  
the capacity of soil to provide essential nutrients in adequate amounts for plant growth. 
 
 
 
 
Key Concepts/Processes/Terms

16 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
Sub-Topic 2.2.1 Soil Fertility (Nutrient Cycles) 
Introduction 
Soil fertility is the ability of soil to supply nutrients required for plant growth. Nutrient cycles involve the continuous recycling of 
nutrients between the soil, plants, animals and the environment, helping maintain soil productivity. In this subtopic, learners will 
appreciate the importance of nutrients for sustaining plant growth and consequently productivity of soils. 
Specific Competence.  
Demonstrate understanding of nutrient cycles.  
Learning Activities 
The following are the activities you can use to teach learners about soil fertility and nutrient cycles. 
2.1.1 Analysing the nutrient cycles 
Learning Materials: Textbooks, charts of nutrient cycles, worksheets, soil samples, compost manure… 
Activity 1. Analysing the nutrient cycles 
Learning materials: Notebooks 
Procedure 
i. 
Divide learners into small groups of 3-4.  
ii. 
Ask learners to visit a school garden or nearby farming area.  
iii. 
Ask learners to observe the general appearance of growing crops or existing vegetation using their previous knowledge on 
soil fertility.

17 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
iv. 
Ask learners to deduce their observations in relation to nutrients of growth. 
v. 
Ask learners to present their findings. 
Content Tip:  
 Emphasis should be on how the plants are benefiting from the nutrient cycles (nitrogen, carbon…). 
2.1.1 Analysing the nutrient cycles 
Activity 2: Scenario  
 A farmer in Kaputa district, Northern province experienced poor yields in crop production during the 2024/2025 farming season. 
Leaching was found to be one of the factors that led to low yields as observed by a local agricultural extension officer that visited his 
farm. The farmer was advised to do the following to improve the crop yields; 
 Practising crop rotation 
 Intercropping 
 Use of organic manure 
Procedure: 
i. 
Divide learners into small groups of 3-4 
ii. 
Ask learners to discuss the advice the farmer was given to improve crop yields. 
iii. 
Ask learners to present their findings. 
Supporting activities 
       i. Consulting digital and written media.  
2.1.2 Illustrating the nitrogen and the carbon cycles  
Learning Materials: Charts, markers,

18 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
Activity 1. Illustrating the nitrogen and the carbon cycles  
Procedure: 
i. Divide learners into small groups of 3-4 
ii. Ask learners to draw a nitrogen cycle diagram showing (atmosphere, soil, plants, animals and bacteria). Learners should show 
the correct sequence of the following processes (nitrogen fixation, nitrification, assimilation, ammonification and 
denitrification). 
iii. Ask learners to discuss the importance of these processes in step (ii) above. 
iv. Ask learners to present their findings. 
Content Tip 
 This activity helps learners to develop practical skills, teamwork, observation and understanding of how nutrient cycles 
maintain soil fertility.  
 
2.1.2 Illustrating the nitrogen and the carbon cycles 
Learning Materials: Charts, markers,  
Activity 2. Illustrating the Nitrogen and the Carbon cycles 
Procedure:

19 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
i) 
Divide learners into small groups of 4 – 5. 
ii) 
Ask learners to design models of the nutrient cycles (Nitrogen cycle and carbon cycle). 
iii) 
Ask learners to identify and label the main processes in each cycle.  
iv) 
Ask learners discuss how nutrients move between soil, plants and animals. 
v) 
Ask learners to present their findings  
 
Supporting activities 
 Viewing video clips on nutrient cycles. 
 Consulting electronic or printed materials 
 
2.1.3 Applying the nitrogen and carbon cycles in relation to soil fertility 
Learning Materials: Dry leaves, straw, paper, green leaves, kitchen waste, manure 
Activity 1. Applying the nitrogen and carbon cycles in relation to soil fertility 
Procedure 
i. Divide learners into small groups of 3-4 
ii. Ask learners to create a compost heap using carbon sources (dry leaves, straw, paper), nitrogen sources (green leaves, kitchen 
waste, manure). 
iii. Ask learners to monitor rate of decomposition, temperature and moisture content. 
iv. Ask learners to explain how composting improves soil carbon and nitrogen content in relation to soil fertility. 
v. Ask learners to present their findings. 
Supporting activities 
 Viewing video clips on nutrient cycles.

20 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
 Consulting electronic or printed materials 
Skills Developed 
 Communicating –  
 
 
Learners working together to share their findings on nutrient cycles 
 Collaborating –  
 
 
Learners tackling given tasks in groups 
 Analytically Thinking –  
 
Analysing the transformation of various compounds into required nutrients. 
 Problem Solving –   
 
Learners using knowledge of nutrient cycles to solve soil fertility problems 
 Environmental Sustainability –  
Efficient utilisation of various nutrients 
 Creating and Innovating –   
Learners making compost  
Expected Standard 
Understanding of nutrient cycles demonstrated accordingly.  
Assessment 
The Provincial Department of Agriculture has organized a provincial conference titled “Future of Soil: Maintaining Its Fertility for 
Future Productivity.” The conference aims to promote awareness of soil management and sustainable agricultural practices among 
communities, farmers, and learners. 
As learners who have acquired knowledge about nutrient cycles, particularly the nitrogen cycle and carbon cycle, your school has 
been invited to participate in the conference. You are required to prepare key points that will help participants understand how soil

Understanding of external and internal morphology of plants demonstrated appropriately 
Assessment 
Task 1 
A school agriculture club has started a garden demonstration to help learners understand plant growth and crop production. The club 
intends to grow different crops, observe plant structures, and practice plant propagation techniques to improve crop production in the 
community. As a learner with knowledge of crop classification, plant morphology, and plant propagation, you have been asked to 
guide other club members on how plants grow and reproduce. 
1. Classify crops according to their life cycles and give examples of each type; 
2. Describe the external and internal morphology of plants, highlighting the main parts and their roles in plant growth; 
3. Draw and label a plant and explain how the structure of each part relates to its function; 
4. Identify suitable conditions required for plant growth and development, and 
5. Explain and demonstrate different methods of vegetative propagation that can be used in the demonstration garden. 
Sub-Topic 2.5.1 Summary 
In this sub-topic, learners developed understanding of plant growth, structure, and reproduction in crop production. It emphasizes the 
ability to classify crops, analyze plant structures, relate plant parts to their functions, identify suitable growth conditions, and practice 
vegetative propagation techniques used in agriculture. This sub-topic helped learners to build practical agricultural knowledge and 
scientific understanding of plant growth and reproduction, which are essential for improving crop production and sustainable farming 
practices.

Sub-Topic 2.5.2 Field Crop Production 
Introduction 
This subtopic focuses on field crop production with particular emphasis on the cultivation of cereal crops. Learners will explore the 
types of cereal crops commonly grown locally and gain an understanding of how to select suitable sites for successful cereal crop 
production. The topic also introduces key technical aspects such as determining plant population, calculating seed germination 
percentage and estimating the required inputs per hectare. In addition, learners will examine essential crop management practices 
throughout the production cycle, from planting to harvesting. Finally, learners will explore innovative approach and improved practices 
aimed at increasing productivity and enhancing the sustainability of cereal crop production. 
 
Specific Competence 
Grow cereal crops

Learning Materials: Calculator, Notebook and pen, Measuring tape, seed and fertilizer rates, Chalkboard/flip chart 
Activity 3: Calculating inputs per hectare 
Scenario 
Given that the following inputs are required in the amounts stated below 
 Maize seed rate: 25 kg per hectare 
 Fertilizer rate: 200 kg per hectare 
Group Activity 
Divide learners into small groups and give them questions such as: 
Example 1: 
A farmer has 2 hectares of land. 
How much maize seed will be needed if the rate is 25 kg/ha? 
Calculation: 
Maize seed needed per hectare = 25 kg × 2 ha = 50 kg of seed 
Example 2: 
A farmer wants to apply 200 kg of fertilizer per hectare on 3 hectares. 
Calculation: 
Fertilizer required for 3 hectares = 200 kg × 3  = 600 kg fertilizer

Task 1 
In a rural community, farmers are focusing on improving cereal crop production to ensure food security and generate income. Locally 
grown cereals such as maize, sorghum, and millet are widely cultivated. Farmers are seeking ways to optimize production through 
improved practices from land preparation to marketing. As a learner with knowledge of field crop production and farm management, 
you are invited to prepare a practical and analytical report for a community agricultural workshop. 
1. Identify locally grown cereal crops in your area and describe their importance; 
2. Select a suitable site for cereal crop production, explaining the factors considered in your selection; 
3. Design a simple crop rotation plan that includes at least one cereal crop and one legume crop; 
4. Determine the appropriate plant population and assess seed germination rates for your selected cereal crop; 
5. Explain how to manage maize or any other locally grown cereal crop from planting to harvesting, including practices such as 
weeding, fertilization, and pest control; 
6. Describe methods of adding value, storing, and marketing the harvested cereal crop, and 
7. Analyze crop yields and suggest innovative ways to increase productivity on the farm. 
Task 2 
A farmer in Nakuyewa community has harvested a large quantity of maize and sorghum. Some of the grain is getting spoiled because 
it is not properly stored, and the farmer is also selling the cereals at a very low price without processing them. As a learner of 
Agricultural Science, explain how you would help the farmer by:

i. Suggesting two ways of adding value to the cereal crops; 
ii. Describing two proper storage methods to prevent damage and losses, and 
iii. Explaining two ways the farmer can market the cereal products to earn more income. 
Sub-Topic 2.5.2 Summary 
Field crop production is the process of growing crops such as cereals, legumes and oil crops on a large scale in open fields. It involves 
activities like land preparation, planting, fertilization, pest control and harvesting to obtain good yield and quality produce. Proper 
management practices help increase productivity and ensure food security. 
Topic 2.5 Summary 
This topic focused on crop production. Learners demonstrated an understanding of the external and internal morphology of plants and 
acquired practical skills in cultivating cereal crops, from planting to harvesting. They also explored methods for adding value to 
produce, as well as techniques for storage and marketing cereal crops. Additionally, learners analysed yields and assessed the quality 
of the produce to identify areas for improvement, while investigating innovative approaches to enhance productivity and efficiency in 
crop production.

1.1.2 Preparing a Poultry House for the Arrival of Day-Old Chicks 
Learning Materials: Brooms, disinfectant, water, litter materials (wood shavings or sawdust), feeders, drinkers, heat source 
(charcoal stove, briskets, gas brooder or electric bulb), footbath, thermometer. 
Activity: Preparing a Poultry House for the Arrival of Day-Old Chicks 
 
Procedure: 
i. Take learners to a poultry house. 
ii. Learners carry out the following tasks; 
 Clean the poultry house by removing old litter and dirt, 
 Wash and disinfect the floor, walls, feeders, and drinkers, 
 Spread dry litter material evenly on the floor, 
 Arrange feeders and drinkers properly in the house, 
 Install a heat source for brooding, 
 Place a footbath with disinfectant at the entrance, 
 Check ventilation and ensure the house is leak-proof and vermin-free, 
 Learners check that the house is warm and ready before chicks arrive. 
iii. Ask learners to discuss why each preparation step is important 
iv. Learners should present what they have discussed 
 
Content Tip 
 This activity should enable learners to understand and practice the correct preparation of a poultry house before introducing day-
old chicks. Learners will be able to correctly prepare a poultry house to ensure a safe and healthy environment for day-old chicks.

110

6.1.3 Formulating feed for broilers 
Learning Materials: Feed ingredients (Maize bran, Maize meal number 3, Soyabean Meal, Fishmeal, Meat and bone meal, Salt, 
dicalcium phosphate, broiler vitamin and mineral premix), broiler feed standards, spade, buckets or basins or clean floor. 
Activity: Formulating feed for broilers 
Procedure:  
i. Divide learners into 3 groups 
ii. Ask learners to calculate the proportions of ingredients required to make a simple broiler formula (Pearson Square method 
should be used to balance ingredients) example (Crushed maize 60%, Soya meal 25%, Fishmeal 10%, Lime 3%, Premix 2%) 
iii. Give each group ingredients required to make a particular broiler feed (starter, grower or finisher feed)  
iv. Ask the learners in groups thoroughly mix the ingredients 
v. Learners should observe the colour, texture and smell of the finished feed 
vi. Store mixed feed in clean bags or containers  
Content Tip 
 The teacher should emphasize calculating the right ratios of ingredients so that the nutrient levels in the formulated feed match 
the animal requirements.  
Discussion question 
i. Learners explain why each ingredient is included in broiler feed 
ii. Discuss the importance of balanced nutrition

6.1.4 Carrying Out Routine Management Practices in Broiler Rearing 
Learning Materials needed: 
Broiler chickens, feeders, drinkers, broiler feed, clean water, litter material, disinfectant, record book, weighing scale. 
Activity: Carrying Out Routine Management Practices in Broiler Rearing 
Procedure: 
i. Divide learners into small groups and assign them to a broiler unit. 
ii. Place day old chicks in the brooder and manage them 
iii. Vaccinate birds following an approved vaccination schedule  
iv. Each group performs routine management practices such as: 
v. Feeding broiler chicken with the correct type of feed from day old to market weight 
vi. Providing clean drinking water throughout the rearing period 
vii. Cleaning and refilling feeders and drinkers. 
viii. 
Checking and replacing wet litter. 
ix. Observing birds for signs of disease or stress. 
x. Recording mortality and feed consumption. 
xi. Learners also check ventilation, temperature, and general cleanliness of the poultry house. 
xii. After the activity, each group reports the tasks performed and observations made. 
Content Tip 
 
 To enable learners to practice routine management activities required for proper growth and health of broiler chickens. 
Discussion Questions

6.1.5 Adding value and marketing (dressing, packaging, smoking of broiler chickens, storage and marketing) 
Learning materials: Live or dressed broiler chickens, Knives and cutting boards, Weighing scale, Packaging materials, Labels and 
markers, Cooler box or refrigerator, Cleaning materials e.g. water, soap, gloves, Charts, Record books or work sheets, Market price 
information. 
Activity 5. Adding Value and Marketing (dressing packaging, smoking of broiler chickens, storage, Marketing). 
Procedure 
i. Divide learners into small groups of 4-5. 
ii. Provide each group with a slaughtered broiler chicken and the required materials (knives, cutting boards, and packaging 
materials, weighing scale ...) 
iii. Ask learners to wash and prepare chickens under hygiene conditions.  
iv. Ask learners to cut the chicken into different portions (breasts, thighs, wings, and drumsticks) to add value. 
v. Ask learners to weigh the different portions using a weighing scale. 
vi. Ask learners to package the chicken portions properly using plastic bags. 
vii. Ask learners to label the packages with product name and price. 
viii. 
Ask each group to calculate the possible selling price and expected profit. 
ix. Let learners present their products and marketing ideas to the class. 
x. Conclude the activity with a class discussion on value addition and good marketing in poultry production. 
Content Tips 
 Maintain good hygiene. 
 Handle knives carefully. 
 Cut the chicken into uniform portions.

 Use clean and suitable packaging. 
 Label the products clearly. 
 Consider market demand and prices. 
 Store the processed chicken properly. 
Supporting Activities 
 Consulting digital resources and print media 
 
6.1.6 Analysing yields and quality of the produce for possible improvement 
Learning Materials: Live or slaughtered broiler chicken, Knives and cutting boards 
Weighing scale, measuring chart/ record sheets, Protective clothing, Sample containers or trays, Calculator, Charts- showing quality 
grades of broiler meat, Pens, note books, Cleaning materials 
Activity 6: Analysing yield and quality of the produce for possible improvement 
Procedure 
i. 
Divide learners into small groups of 4–5. 
ii. 
Provide each group with a slaughtered broiler chicken and the required materials (knives, cutting boards, packaging materials, 
weighing scale, etc.). 
iii. 
Ask learners to observe the broiler chicken and record its general condition e.g. weight and appearance 
iv. 
Ask learners to weigh the live broiler chicken 
v. 
Slaughter and dress the chicken (ask learners to do this) 
vi. 
Weigh the dressed chicken-record the weight to determine the weight to determine the dressing percentage yield. 
vii. 
Ask learners to cut the chicken into parts such as breast, wings, thighs and drumsticks.

114 
 
AGRICULTURAL SCIENCE TEACHING MODULE FORM 2 TERM 1 & 2 
viii. 
Ask learners to weigh the different parts and record the results for comparison. 
ix. 
Ask learners to observe the quality of the meat-noting factors such as color, tenderness, fat distribution and cleanliness. 
x. 
Ask learners to discuss within the groups, the factors that may affect yield and quality 
xi. 
Ask learners to present group findings to the class and suggest ways to improve broiler production and meat quality 
Content Tip 
 Factors that may affect yield and quality include: 
 Breed – some breeds grow faster and produce more meat. 
 Management – good feeding, vaccination and proper housing affect meat quality. 
 Health – disease reduces body weight and value. 
 Slaughter age – birds slaughtered at the correct age give good meat. 
 Practice proper handling and hygiene. 
 Use improved broiler breeds with high growth rate. 
 Provide balanced and adequate feed to promote growth. 
 Vaccinate and treat birds to control diseases. 
 Maintain good housing, sanitation and ventilation 
Supporting Activities 
i. Viewing video clips  
ii. Consulting electronic or printed media

Assessment 
Task 1 
A group of learners at Chingola Secondary School raised 100 broiler chickens for sale. After 6 weeks, they slaughtered the birds and 
discovered that some chickens were smaller in size, while others had good body weight and quality meat. The learners also realized that 
selling whole birds at the local market brought less profit than expected. Their teacher asked them to think of better ways to improve 
production, increase quality, and earn more money from the broilers. 
As a group: 
i. Suggest two ways of adding value to the broiler chickens before selling them; 
ii. Explain two methods learners can use to analyze the yield and quality of the broiler meat; 
iii. Identify three possible reasons why some broilers had low body weight; 
iv. Suggest three innovative ways the learners can use to increase productivity in the next batch of broilers, and 
v. Propose two marketing strategies that can help the learners sell their broiler products at a better price. 
Task 2 
In a rural community, a poultry enterprise has been established to rear broiler chickens for meat production. The enterprise aims to 
train learners and farmers on modern poultry management practices to ensure high productivity, profitability, and quality meat supply. 
As a learner with knowledge of broiler production and farm management, you have been invited to prepare a practical report and 
presentation for a community poultry workshop. 
i. Explain how to manage broiler chickens from day-old chicks to market weight, including feeding, watering, health care, and 
environmental management;

118

ii. Describe how to prepare a poultry house for the arrival of day-old chicks, including cleaning, disinfecting, bedding, and setting 
up feeders and drinkers; 
iii. Formulate appropriate feed for broiler chickens at different growth stages; 
iv. Carry out routine management practices such as feeding, watering, vaccination, and monitoring bird health; 
v. Explain methods of adding value and marketing broiler chickens, including packaging, pricing, and customer engagement; 
vi. Analyse yields and quality of the produce to identify areas for improvement, and 
vii. Explore innovative ways of increasing broiler productivity, such as improved housing, feed efficiency, and biosecurity 
measures.

This topic focused on broiler production. Learners developed an understanding of the anatomy and physiology of poultry and applied 
this knowledge in the practical rearing of broiler chickens to maturity. Throughout the process, they considered and applied 
appropriate management practices, including proper feeding, housing, hygiene, and health care, to ensure successful broiler 
production.
